metadata.notes: Wingspan standard overhaul: removed legacy sections format, added rules_citations with key rulings, validated content against official rulebook.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Boards & Mats
Let's start with the boards. You'll find 1 large game board — it shows 4 advancement tracks running along the edges (Exploration, Science, Technology, and Military) plus a central territory map where hexagonal tiles will be placed throughout the game.

There are 16 civilization mats, each with a unique asymmetric ability — these are what make every game feel different. Each player also gets 1 income mat, which tracks your income across 5 eras with rows of symbols that unlock as you place buildings. Finally, there are 5 capital city mats, each with a unique 9-column grid where you'll place landmarks and buildings — think of it as a little city-building puzzle within the bigger game.

#### Cards
The 43 tapestry cards are played at the start of each new era and give you ongoing or immediate benefits — these shape your strategy for the era ahead.

The 48 territory tiles are hexagonal pieces you'll gain from the Exploration track and place on the central map.

The 33 tech cards are double-sided — basic on one side, upgraded on the other. You gain them from the Science track and flip them when you hit upgrade spaces.

#### Miniatures & Buildings
The 18 landmark miniatures are the showpiece of the box — 3D plastic buildings in various shapes that you earn from advancement tracks and place on your capital city grid. They come in shapes like L-pieces, T-pieces, and straight bars.

Each player also gets 13 income buildings — small wooden cubes in your player color. When you gain these from the Technology track, you place them on your income mat to expose new income symbols.

#### Tokens & Resources
You'll find resource tokens in 4 types: Food (green), Workers (orange/brown), Gold (yellow), and Culture (purple/blue). You can hold up to 8 of each — any excess is lost.

There are VP tokens in denominations of 1, 5, 10, and 20. Each player gets marker tokens for the 4 advancement tracks plus 1 score marker.

#### Other
The box also includes 1 rulebook, 1 reference guide, 5 player reference cards, and a first-player token.

subtopics.title: Initial Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Setting Up the Game
Place the game board in the center of the table. Find the 18 landmark miniatures and place each one on its marked space along the advancement tracks. These are first-come, first-served rewards — when you reach a landmark space, you take that miniature.

Shuffle the territory tiles face-down into a supply pile near the board.

Shuffle the tapestry cards and tech cards into two separate face-down decks.

Sort the income buildings by type and place them in a supply near the board.

Now deal each player 2 civilization mats — look at both, choose the one you want to play, and return the other face-down. Each player also takes 1 income mat and 1 capital city mat.

Deal 3 tapestry cards and 3 tech cards to each player. Keep these in your hand.

Place your player markers at the start (bottom) of all 4 advancement tracks on the board.

#### First Income Turn
Before the game begins, all players simultaneously take their first income turn. You gain resources from the leftmost column on your income mat — this gives you your starting economy. Look at what you received: a handful of Food, Workers, Gold, and Culture. This is what you'll spend to advance in Era 1.

Some civilizations modify your setup or starting resources — if yours does, resolve that now.

Randomly determine the first player.

At this point, you should have your civilization mat chosen, your income mat and capital city mat in front of you, markers at the start of all 4 tracks, 3 tapestry cards and 3 tech cards in hand, and your starting resources from the first income turn.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### What You Do on Your Turn
On your turn, you do exactly one of two things. This simple choice is the heartbeat of the entire game.

**Option 1: Take an Advance Turn**
Pick any of the 4 advancement tracks and pay the resource cost shown on the next space up. Move your marker up one space and immediately gain the benefit printed on that space. If you land on a landmark space, take that 3D miniature right away and place it on your capital city mat.

You can advance on any track in any order across your turns — there's no requirement to stay on one track or alternate between them.

**Option 2: Take an Income Turn**
When you can't advance (you're out of resources) or choose not to, you take an income turn instead. This is a bigger deal than it sounds — here's what happens:

First, play a tapestry card from your hand. This is optional but very important — tapestry cards give you bonuses for the upcoming era. Then activate your civilization ability if it triggers on income turns. Finally, gain income from all the exposed symbols on your income mat.

Taking an income turn advances you to the next era. You'll take exactly 5 income turns over the course of the game (one per era).

#### The Pace Is Asynchronous
Here's something unique about Tapestry: players don't need to be in the same era. You might take your 3rd income turn while other players are still advancing in Era 2. This is normal — the game ends when ALL players have taken their 5th and final income turn. Players who finish early wait for others to complete their turns.

#### Important Timing Rules
Tapestry cards are played BEFORE you gain income — so you can't use the resources you're about to receive to pay for the card's costs.

Landmarks must be placed on your capital city immediately when earned — plan ahead so you don't block important rows and columns.

Income buildings must also be placed on your income mat immediately when gained.

subtopics.title: The Four Advancement Tracks
subtopics.content:
#### Exploration Track
This track is your gateway to the territory map. As you advance, you gain hexagonal territory tiles to place on the central map. Some spaces let you explore face-down (draw a random tile), while others let you choose from available face-up tiles.

When you place a tile, position it adjacent to existing tiles on the map. Many tiles have resources printed on them — you collect those immediately when you place the tile. Territory tiles expand the shared map and can earn you control bonuses.

The primary resource cost for this track is Food.

#### Science Track
This track is about technology. As you advance, you gain tech cards and reach upgrade spaces where you flip tech cards from their basic side to their powerful upgraded side. Upgraded tech cards provide strong ongoing abilities or end-game VP.

This track works best as a mid-game investment — once you know what upgrades would help your strategy most, push into Science to get them.

The primary resource cost for this track is Workers.

#### Technology Track
Don't confuse this with Science — the Technology track is about your economic engine. As you advance here, you gain income buildings to place on your income mat. Each building placed exposes new income symbols, which means better income on every future income turn.

This is the most important track for building your economy. The income buildings come in 5 types matching the 5 columns on your income mat. Getting buildings early means they compound across multiple eras.

The primary resource cost for this track is Gold.

#### Military Track
This track gives you outposts to place on territories and the ability to conquer opponent-controlled territories. Conquering lets you topple an opponent's outpost on an adjacent territory — their outpost stays but is tipped over.

Military advances give you direct VP and map control. This track is strongest in the mid-to-late game when the map has plenty of territories to contest.

The primary resource cost for this track is Culture.

subtopics.title: Capital City & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Your Capital City Mat
Your capital city mat is a grid with 9 columns where you'll place landmarks and income buildings throughout the game. Think of it as a spatial puzzle — you're trying to completely fill rows and columns.

Landmarks are the 3D miniatures you earn from advancement tracks. They come in specific shapes — some are 1x1, some are L-shaped, some are T-shaped, and some are long bars. These bigger pieces are harder to fit.

Income buildings are simple 1x1 cubes that fill single spaces. Use them to patch gaps that landmarks can't reach.

Pieces cannot overlap each other or extend outside the grid boundaries.

#### How City Scoring Works
Here's the key rule: each completely filled row scores the VP value shown at that row's edge. Each completely filled column scores the VP shown at the column's top. But partially completed rows and columns score absolutely zero — even one empty space means no points for that line.

This makes city scoring very all-or-nothing. Completing rows AND columns is the key to a high capital city score.

#### Placement Strategy
Place landmarks first — they're bigger and harder to fit, so they need to go down before you fill gaps with 1x1 income buildings. Study your specific capital city mat during setup, because each one has a different grid pattern with pre-filled spaces.

A common mistake: placing landmarks carelessly early on, then realizing they've blocked you from completing a high-value row later. Plan before you place.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Final Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### When Does the Game End?
The game ends when all players have taken their 5th and final income turn. Because of the asynchronous pacing, some players will finish before others — if you're done early, you wait for everyone else to complete their turns.

#### Adding Up Your Score
Your final score comes from several sources:

**VP tokens** earned during the game — from track advances, tapestry card effects, conquest bonuses, tech card abilities, and civilization powers.

**Capital city scoring** — Count up the VP for every completely filled row and column on your capital city grid. Remember: partial rows and columns score zero.

**Upgraded tech card bonuses** — Some upgraded tech cards award end-game VP based on what you've accomplished.

**Tapestry card bonuses** — Some tapestry cards give VP based on the final game state.

**Territory control** — Points for territories you control, depending on your civilization's abilities.

#### Determining the Winner
The player with the most total VP wins. If there's a tie, the player with the most remaining resources wins.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Starting Principles

**Focus on 2 tracks, not 4.** Spreading your resources across all 4 advancement tracks dilutes everything. Pick 2 tracks that synergize with each other and push deep. The further you climb on a track, the better the rewards get — and the landmarks waiting at the top are worth the push.

**The Technology track is king for beginners.** Income buildings from the Technology track are how you grow your economy. Every income building you place exposes new income symbols on your mat, which means better income on every future income turn. Getting income buildings early is the single best thing you can do — they compound across every remaining era.

**Don't take income turns too early.** Every income turn means one fewer era of advancing. If you take income on turn 3 while another player waits until turn 8, they'll have 5 more advances than you. Stretch your resources as far as possible before taking income — but don't wait so long that you're making useless advances.

**Plan your capital city placement from the start.** Before placing landmarks and income buildings, look at the shapes coming and think about where they'll fit. Try to complete full rows and columns — remember, partial rows score zero. A common mistake: placing your first landmark wherever it fits, then realizing it blocks a high-value row later.

**Save strong tapestry cards for later eras.** A tapestry card that gives "2 VP per advance this era" scores 4 VP if you advance twice — but 12 VP if you advance six times. Your engine is bigger in later eras, so your tapestry cards become much more valuable when you play them later.

**Gold is your emergency fund.** Don't spend gold frivolously. Save it for when you're one resource short of a critical advance — that flexibility is worth more than spending it early.

**Read your civilization ability carefully.** Your civilization power shapes your entire strategy. If your civ rewards Exploration, lean into that track. If it rewards tech upgrades, push Science. The best players build their whole game plan around their civ ability.

#### Common Beginner Mistakes
- Taking income too early — you lose potential advance turns that build your engine
- Ignoring the Technology track — income buildings ARE your economic engine
- Placing landmarks poorly on the capital city grid, blocking future rows and columns
- Spreading evenly across all 4 tracks instead of focusing on 2
- Not reading tapestry card timing — some say "this era" and some say "when played," and the difference matters
- Hoarding resources instead of advancing — resources don't score directly, advances do
- Racing for a landmark that someone else will reach first — pivot to a different track instead of wasting resources

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Tapestry

**Before You Start**
Everyone picks a civilization from the 2 dealt to them. For your first game, look for civilizations with straightforward abilities. Historians (gain VP for each advance) and Craftsmen (extra capital city bonuses) are great starting picks. Avoid civilizations that completely change the rules — like The Chosen or Inventors — until you understand the base game.

**Era 1: The Setup Income Turn**
Everyone takes their first income turn simultaneously. Here's what happens:

You won't play a tapestry card yet — save them for later eras when they're more impactful. Gain income from the leftmost column of your income mat — this gives you your starting resources. Check your civilization ability — some give bonus resources or cards at setup.

Look at what you've got: a handful of Food, Workers, Gold, and Culture, plus 3 tapestry cards and 3 tech cards in your hand.

**Your First Advance Turns**
Now the real game begins. Going clockwise, each player takes one turn at a time.

Look at the bottom of each advancement track — the first few spaces are cheap, often costing just 1 resource. Here's a solid opening plan:

*Turn 1*: Advance on the Technology track. Pay the cost and gain an income building — a small wooden cube in your color. Place it immediately on your income mat. You just unlocked better income for your next income turn.

*Turn 2*: Advance on your second focus track. Exploration is great for beginners — you gain territory tiles with free resources printed on them. Place the tile on the central map and collect the bonus.

*Turn 3*: Continue advancing on one of your two focus tracks. If a landmark is within reach (1-2 more spaces away), consider pushing for it. Landmarks are first-come, first-served — if someone else gets there first, it's gone.

**The Landmark Race**
When you reach a landmark space on a track, take that 3D building miniature and place it on your capital city mat right away. Landmarks are big and oddly shaped, so think carefully about where they'll go. Try to position them where they help complete full rows and columns on your grid.

**When to Take Your Era 2 Income Turn**
When you're running low on resources and can't make meaningful advances, it's time for income. On your income turn:

Play a tapestry card if you have a good one for this era. Activate your civilization ability. Then collect income from all exposed symbols on your income mat — this should be noticeably better than Era 1 thanks to the income buildings you placed.

**Eras 3-5: The Engine Grows**
Each era, your income should grow thanks to more income buildings. Use your growing resource base to advance further on your focus tracks. The sweet spot is 4-6 advances per era.

Late-game tapestry cards become huge. A card like "Gain 1 VP for each space you've advanced on any track" might be worth 15-30 VP in Era 5.

**The Final Income Turn**
Your 5th income turn ends your game. Score your capital city grid — only completely filled rows and columns count. Add up all your VP tokens and count any end-game bonuses from tech cards and tapestry cards.

**First Game Target Score**: Aim for 150-200 VP. Don't worry about winning — focus on understanding the advance-then-income rhythm and how income buildings power your entire engine.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Civilization Tier Guide

Tapestry's civilizations are famously unbalanced — Stonemaier released official adjustments after community data showed significant win-rate gaps. Here's where each civ falls:

**Tier S (Overpowered pre-nerf):** Futurists had a 79% win rate at 3 players before nerfs. Isolationists score for your largest connected landmass — very strong when combining Exploration with Military.

**Tier A (Strong):** Historians gain benefits when opponents gain landmarks — they scale with player count and reward passive play. Craftsmen can score 30+ VP from civ mat scoring alone. Merrymakers have straightforward civ mat scoring that's hard to mess up.

**Tier B (Solid):** Militants reward Military focus. Traders offer resource flexibility that gets better at 3-5 players. Inventors get extra tech upgrades. The Chosen synergize with Science.

**Tier C (Situational):** Mystics have high variance. Architects are strong at 2 players but weak at 5. Nomads need both Exploration and Military to work.

#### Track Pairings & Resource Costs
Each track has a primary resource cost: Exploration costs Food, Science costs Workers, Technology costs Gold, and Military costs Culture.

The best pairings: Exploration + Military (place tiles then conquer them), Technology + Exploration (engine building plus free resources from tiles), and Science as a mid-game accelerator added to any 2-track base.

#### The Income Building Secret
This is critical to understand: only the 1st, 3rd, and 4th income buildings per type generate future income. The 2nd building is a dead placement unless you get the 3rd out before your next income turn.

The single highest-impact skill in Tapestry: get 2-3 income buildings out BEFORE your first income turn. Each early building compounds roughly 3 extra resources over the course of the game.

#### The Exploration Rush
The strongest known opening: advance Exploration 4 times. Territory tiles give you resources when placed, making many of these advances net-zero cost. Then advance Military twice — conquer for resources and pick up a 2nd income building. This gets 2 buildings out before your first income turn, giving you a huge economic lead.

#### Player Count Adjustments
- **2 Players**: Both players have room to fill their cities — city strategy dominates. Military is weak because there's less to conquer.
- **3-4 Players**: Landmark racing becomes real — plan your track choices around which landmarks are still available. Historians get strong here.
- **5 Players**: City completion is less reliable with more landmark competition. Military and territory control strategies dominate because the map gets crowded.

#### Key Decisions by Era
1. **Era 1**: Get income buildings placed before your first income turn — this is the highest-impact decision in the game.
2. **Era 2-3**: Commit to your 2 focus tracks and push deep.
3. **Era 3**: The pivotal decision — one more advance versus taking income? Getting this timing right separates good players from great ones.
4. **Era 4**: Play your best tapestry card — this is when they have the highest impact.
5. **Era 5**: Close out capital city rows for 10+ VP each. Every action must score.

key_rulings.text: When you reach a landmark space on an advancement track, you take that landmark miniature. If another player has already taken it, you do not receive a landmark. Each landmark can only be claimed by one player.
On an income turn, you first play a tapestry card (optional), then activate your civilization ability, and then gain income. The tapestry card is played BEFORE you receive your income resources.

key_rulings.topic: Asynchronous pacing — players in different eras
key_rulings.text: Players do not need to be in the same era. One player may take their 3rd income turn while others are still advancing. The game ends only when ALL players have completed their 5th income turn.

key_rulings.topic: Income buildings must be placed immediately
key_rulings.text: When you gain an income building from an advancement track, you must place it on your income mat immediately. You cannot hold it in reserve.

key_rulings.topic: Capital city partial rows score zero
key_rulings.text: Only fully completed rows and columns on your capital city mat score victory points. A row or column with even one empty space scores zero points.

key_rulings.topic: Gold is not a wild resource
key_rulings.text: Gold is one of the four resource types (Food, Workers, Gold, Culture). It is not wild and cannot substitute for other resources unless a specific ability allows it.

key_rulings.topic: Landmarks must be placed on capital city immediately
key_rulings.text: When you gain a landmark from an advancement track, you must place it on your capital city mat immediately. Plan ahead — a poorly placed landmark can block future rows and columns.

key_rulings.topic: Conquer dice — choose result in later eras
key_rulings.text: When conquering via the Military track, you roll the conquer die. In the first era, you must take the rolled result. In later eras, you may choose between the rolled result and VP.
You can have at most 8 of each resource (coins, workers, food, and culture are resources). Any resources gained beyond 8 of a single type are lost. Victory points are NOT resources and have no cap.

key_rulings.topic: No actions after your final income turn
key_rulings.text: You may not respond to other players (e.g., tapestry cards) after your final income turn. Your active game is over. You may still passively gain VP from civilization abilities that trigger on other players' actions, but you cannot actively play cards or spend resources.

key_rulings.topic: Benefits and landmarks are mandatory; bonuses are optional
key_rulings.text: All bonuses in the game are optional. Benefits and landmarks are mandatory rewards. When you advance on a track, you must gain the benefit shown on the space (if possible) and must take any landmark earned. However, bonuses (the extra cost/reward shown below certain spaces) are always optional.

key_rulings.topic: Cannot activate same track space twice per turn
key_rulings.text: You may not activate the same space on an advancement track more than once during your turn. If civilization abilities or card effects push you back and allow you to advance again, you cannot re-activate a space already visited this turn.

key_rulings.topic: Conquer and topple — territory with 2 tokens is permanently unconquerable
key_rulings.text: When you conquer a territory controlled by an opponent, you place your outpost and topple the opponent's outpost. A territory with 2 tokens (in any combination of standing/toppled) cannot be conquered again by anyone. A toppled outpost still counts for adjacency purposes.

The income turn must be executed in order: 1) Activate civilization ability, 2) Play a tapestry card (skip on first income turn), 3) Upgrade one technology card (optional), 4) Score VP from exposed icons, 5) Gain income from exposed resource icons. Performing steps out of order is a common mistake.
